title: Hello-Chat: Towards Realistic Social Audio Interactions
summary: Recent advancements in Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance in speech recognition and translation. However, existing models often suffer from a disconnect between perception and expression, resulting in a robotic "read-speech" style that lacks the spontaneity and emotional resonance of real human interaction. In this report, we introduce Hello-Chat, an end-to-end audio language model designed for realistic social scenarios. By leveraging a massive dataset of real-life conversations and employing a modality-interleaved training strategy, Hello-Chat achieves a breakthrough in anthropomorphic generation. Experimental results show that our model not only reaches state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on specific audio understanding tasks but also significantly outperforms existing baselines in prosodic naturalness and emotional alignment, paving the way for the next generation of empathetic AI agents.

AI.tldr: Hello-Chat is an end-to-end audio language model that addresses the disconnect between perception and expression in existing LALMs, achieving realistic social conversation generation through modality-interleaved training on real-life conversation data.
AI.motivation: Existing Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) suffer from a disconnect between perception and expression, producing robotic "read-speech" style outputs that lack the spontaneity and emotional resonance of real human interaction, limiting their effectiveness in realistic social scenarios.
AI.method: Hello-Chat employs an end-to-end audio language model architecture with modality-interleaved training strategy, leveraging a massive dataset of real-life conversations to achieve anthropomorphic generation capabilities.
AI.result: The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on specific audio understanding tasks and significantly outperforms existing baselines in prosodic naturalness and emotional alignment, demonstrating breakthrough anthropomorphic generation.
AI.conclusion: Hello-Chat represents a significant advancement toward realistic social AI agents, paving the way for the next generation of empathetic AI systems capable of natural, emotionally resonant conversations.

title: Leveraging large multimodal models for audio-video deepfake detection: a pilot study
summary: Audio-visual deepfake detection (AVD) is increasingly important as modern generators can fabricate convincing speech and video. Most current multimodal detectors are small, task-specific models: they work well on curated tests but scale poorly and generalize weakly across domains. We introduce AV-LMMDetect, a supervised fine-tuned (SFT) large multimodal model that casts AVD as a prompted yes/no classification - "Is this video real or fake?". Built on Qwen 2.5 Omni, it jointly analyzes audio and visual streams for deepfake detection and is trained in two stages: lightweight LoRA alignment followed by audio-visual encoder full fine-tuning. On FakeAVCeleb and Mavos-DD, AV-LMMDetect matches or surpasses prior methods and sets a new state of the art on Mavos-DD datasets.

AI.tldr: AV-LMMDetect is a supervised fine-tuned large multimodal model that frames audio-visual deepfake detection as a prompted yes/no classification task, achieving state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal deepfake detectors are small, task-specific models that work well on curated tests but scale poorly and generalize weakly across domains, necessitating a more robust solution.
AI.method: Built on Qwen 2.5 Omni, AV-LMMDetect casts audio-visual deepfake detection as a prompted yes/no classification ("Is this video real or fake?"). It uses two-stage training: lightweight LoRA alignment followed by audio-visual encoder full fine-tuning to jointly analyze audio and visual streams.
AI.result: On FakeAVCeleb and Mavos-DD datasets, AV-LMMDetect matches or surpasses prior methods and sets a new state of the art on Mavos-DD datasets.
AI.conclusion: AV-LMMDetect demonstrates that large multimodal models can be effectively adapted for audio-visual deepfake detection through supervised fine-tuning, offering improved scalability and generalization compared to small task-specific models.

title: AudioCapBench: Quick Evaluation on Audio Captioning across Sound, Music, and Speech
summary: We introduce AudioCapBench, a benchmark for evaluating audio captioning capabilities of large multimodal models. \method covers three distinct audio domains, including environmental sound, music, and speech, with 1,000 curated evaluation samples drawn from established datasets. We evaluate 13 models across two providers (OpenAI, Google Gemini) using both reference-based metrics (METEOR, BLEU, ROUGE-L) and an LLM-as-Judge framework that scores predictions on three orthogonal dimensions: \textit{accuracy} (semantic correctness), \textit{completeness} (coverage of reference content), and \textit{hallucination} (absence of fabricated content). Our results reveal that Gemini models generally outperform OpenAI models on overall captioning quality, with Gemini~3~Pro achieving the highest overall score (6.00/10), while OpenAI models exhibit lower hallucination rates. All models perform best on speech captioning and worst on music captioning. We release the benchmark as well as evaluation code to facilitate reproducible audio understanding research.

AI.tldr: AudioCapBench is a benchmark for evaluating audio captioning in large multimodal models across environmental sound, music, and speech domains, using both traditional metrics and LLM-as-Judge scoring on accuracy, completeness, and hallucination.
AI.motivation: To establish a standardized benchmark for evaluating audio captioning capabilities of large multimodal models across diverse audio domains, enabling systematic comparison and advancement in audio understanding research.
AI.method: Created a benchmark with 1,000 curated samples from established datasets across three audio domains (environmental sound, music, speech). Evaluated 13 models from OpenAI and Google Gemini using reference-based metrics (METEOR, BLEU, ROUGE-L) and an LLM-as-Judge framework scoring predictions on accuracy (semantic correctness), completeness (coverage), and hallucination (absence of fabricated content).
AI.result: Gemini models generally outperform OpenAI models on overall captioning quality, with Gemini~3~Pro achieving the highest overall score (6.00/10). OpenAI models exhibit lower hallucination rates. All models perform best on speech captioning and worst on music captioning.
AI.conclusion: AudioCapBench provides a comprehensive evaluation framework for audio captioning models, revealing performance patterns across different model families and audio domains, with the benchmark and evaluation code released to facilitate reproducible research in audio understanding.

title: Online Register for Dual-Mode Self-Supervised Speech Models: Mitigating The Lack of Future Context
summary: Dual-mode self-supervised speech models (S3Ms), which jointly pre-trained in the offline and online mode, suffer from attention mismatch in streaming scenarios due to missing future context. To address this challenge, we proposed online registers, learnable tokens appended to each chunk in online mode. These tokens act as virtual placeholders for unseen future frames, enabling the model to compensate for missing context without introducing additional latency. Furthermore, we introduce a future prediction loss that explicitly guides the registers to capture predictive cues, thereby enriching their ability to retain future information. Experiments on LibriSpeech, and out-of-domain benchmarks demonstrate that online registers consistently reduce the performance gap between offline and online modes, achieving a 3.4% relative improvement on LibriSpeech with 160 ms chunks, especially in low-latency settings.

AI.tldr: Online registers - learnable tokens appended to streaming chunks - act as virtual placeholders for missing future context, reducing attention mismatch in dual-mode self-supervised speech models.
AI.motivation: Dual-mode self-supervised speech models suffer from attention mismatch in streaming scenarios due to missing future context, creating performance gap between offline and online modes.
AI.method: Proposed online registers (learnable tokens appended to each chunk in online mode) as virtual placeholders for unseen future frames, plus future prediction loss to guide registers to capture predictive cues.
AI.result: Online registers consistently reduce performance gap between offline and online modes, achieving 3.4% relative improvement on LibriSpeech with 160 ms chunks, especially effective in low-latency settings.
AI.conclusion: Online registers effectively address attention mismatch in streaming scenarios without adding latency, enabling better compensation for missing future context in dual-mode self-supervised speech models.

title: An Empirical Analysis of Task-Induced Encoder Bias in Fréchet Audio Distance
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures. Submitted to Interspeech 2026. Source code and evaluation pipeline are available at: https://github.com/wonwoo-jeong/fad-encoder-bias
summary: Fréchet Audio Distance (FAD) is the de facto standard for evaluating text-to-audio generation, yet its scores depend on the underlying encoder's embedding space. An encoder's training task dictates which acoustic features are preserved or discarded, causing FAD to inherit systematic task-induced biases. We decompose evaluation into Recall, Precision, and Alignment (split into semantic and structural dimensions), using log-scale normalization for fair cross-encoder comparison. Controlled experiments on six encoders across two datasets reveal a four-axis trade-off: reconstruction-based AudioMAE leads precision sensitivity; ASR-trained Whisper dominates structural detection but is blind to signal degradation; classification-trained VGGish maximizes semantic detection but penalizes legitimate intra-class variation. Since no single encoder is a universal evaluator, future metrics must shift toward evaluation-native encoders intrinsically aligned with human perception.

AI.tldr: FAD evaluation biases depend on encoder training tasks; systematic analysis reveals trade-offs across six encoders, showing no universal evaluator exists, necessitating evaluation-native encoders.
AI.motivation: FAD is the standard for text-to-audio evaluation but inherits systematic biases from encoder training tasks, which dictate which acoustic features are preserved or discarded, making cross-encoder comparisons unfair.
AI.method: Decompose evaluation into Recall, Precision, and Alignment (semantic/structural dimensions) with log-scale normalization for fair cross-encoder comparison. Conduct controlled experiments on six encoders across two datasets.
AI.result: Reveals four-axis trade-off: AudioMAE leads precision sensitivity; Whisper dominates structural detection but ignores signal degradation; VGGish maximizes semantic detection but penalizes legitimate intra-class variation. No single encoder serves as universal evaluator.
AI.conclusion: Future metrics must shift toward evaluation-native encoders intrinsically aligned with human perception rather than relying on task-specific encoders with inherent biases.

title: Training with Hard Constraints: Learning Neural Certificates and Controllers for SDEs
summary: Due to their expressive power, neural networks (NNs) are promising templates for functional optimization problems, particularly for reach-avoid certificate generation for systems governed by stochastic differential equations (SDEs). However, ensuring hard-constraint satisfaction remains a major challenge. In this work, we propose two constraint-driven training frameworks with guarantees for supermartingale-based neural certificate construction and controller synthesis for SDEs. The first approach enforces certificate inequalities via domain discretization and a bound-based loss, guaranteeing global validity once the loss reaches zero. We show that this method also enables joint NN controller-certificate synthesis with hard guarantees. For high-dimensional systems where discretization becomes prohibitive, we introduce a partition-free, scenario-based training method that provides arbitrarily tight PAC guarantees for certificate constraint satisfaction. Benchmarks demonstrate scalability of the bound-based method up to 5D, outperforming the state of the art, and scalability of the scenario-based approach to at least 10D with high-confidence guarantees.

AI.tldr: Two neural network-based frameworks for generating reach-avoid certificates and controllers for stochastic differential equations with hard constraint guarantees: a bound-based method using domain discretization for up to 5D systems, and a scenario-based approach with PAC guarantees for high-dimensional systems up to 10D+.
AI.motivation: Neural networks are promising for functional optimization in stochastic control problems, particularly for reach-avoid certificate generation for SDEs, but ensuring hard-constraint satisfaction remains a major challenge that needs to be addressed.
AI.method: Two constraint-driven training frameworks: 1) Bound-based method enforcing certificate inequalities via domain discretization and bound-based loss, guaranteeing global validity when loss reaches zero, enabling joint NN controller-certificate synthesis. 2) Partition-free, scenario-based training method providing arbitrarily tight PAC guarantees for certificate constraint satisfaction in high-dimensional systems where discretization becomes prohibitive.
AI.result: The bound-based method scales up to 5D systems, outperforming state-of-the-art approaches. The scenario-based approach scales to at least 10D systems with high-confidence guarantees, demonstrating superior scalability for high-dimensional stochastic control problems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed frameworks successfully address the challenge of hard-constraint satisfaction in neural certificate construction and controller synthesis for SDEs, providing scalable solutions with formal guarantees for both moderate-dimensional (bound-based) and high-dimensional (scenario-based) stochastic systems.

title: DashengTokenizer: One layer is enough for unified audio understanding and generation
summary: This paper introduces DashengTokenizer, a continuous audio tokenizer engineered for joint use in both understanding and generation tasks. Unlike conventional approaches, which train acoustic tokenizers and subsequently integrate frozen semantic knowledge, our method inverts this paradigm: we leverage frozen semantic features and inject acoustic information. In linear evaluation across 22 diverse tasks, our method outperforms previous audio codec and audio encoder baselines by a significant margin while maintaining competitive audio reconstruction quality. Notably, we demonstrate that this acoustic injection improves performance for tasks such as speech emotion recognition, music understanding, and acoustic scene classification. We further evaluate the tokenizer's generative performance on text-to-audio (TTA), text-to-music (TTM), and speech enhancement (SE). Our approach surpasses standard variational autoencoder (VAE)-based methods on TTA and TTM tasks, while its effectiveness on SE underscores its capabilities as a general-purpose audio encoder. Finally, our results challenge the prevailing assumption that VAE-based architectures are a prerequisite for audio synthesis. Checkpoints are available at https://huggingface.co/mispeech/dashengtokenizer.

AI.tldr: DashengTokenizer is a continuous audio tokenizer that uses frozen semantic features with injected acoustic information, outperforming previous methods on understanding tasks while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality and challenging VAE-based synthesis assumptions.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to develop a unified audio tokenizer for both understanding and generation tasks, challenging the conventional paradigm of training acoustic tokenizers first and then integrating semantic knowledge. The authors propose inverting this approach by leveraging frozen semantic features and injecting acoustic information.
AI.method: DashengTokenizer employs a continuous audio tokenizer architecture that uses frozen semantic features as a foundation and injects acoustic information into this framework. Unlike traditional methods that train acoustic tokenizers and then add semantic knowledge, this approach starts with semantic features and enhances them with acoustic data.
AI.result: In linear evaluation across 22 diverse tasks, DashengTokenizer outperforms previous audio codec and audio encoder baselines by a significant margin while maintaining competitive audio reconstruction quality. It shows particular improvements on speech emotion recognition, music understanding, and acoustic scene classification. For generation tasks, it surpasses standard VAE-based methods on text-to-audio and text-to-music tasks, and demonstrates effectiveness on speech enhancement.
AI.conclusion: DashengTokenizer successfully demonstrates that a continuous audio tokenizer can effectively serve both understanding and generation tasks by leveraging frozen semantic features with injected acoustic information. The results challenge the prevailing assumption that VAE-based architectures are necessary for audio synthesis, showing competitive performance across diverse audio tasks.

title: Data-Driven Linearization based Arc Fault Prediction in Medium Voltage Electrical Distribution System
summary: High-impedance arc faults (HIAFs) in medium-voltage electrical distribution systems are difficult to detect due to their low fault current levels and nonlinear transient behavior. Traditional detection algorithms generally struggle with predictions under dynamic waveform scenarios. This research provides our approach of using a unique data-driven linearization (DDL) framework for early prediction of HIAFs, giving both interpretability and scalability. The proposed method translates nonlinear current waveforms into a linearized space using coordinate embeddings and polynomial transformation, enabling precise modelling of fault precursors.The total duration of the test waveform is 0.5 seconds, within which the arc fault occurs between 0.2 seconds to 0.3 seconds. Our proposed approach using DDL, trained solely on the pre-fault healthy region (0.10 seconds to 0.18 seconds) effectively captures certain invisible fault precursors, to accurately predict the onset of fault at 0.189 seconds, which is approximately 0.011 seconds (i.e., 11 milliseconds) earlier than the actual fault occurrence. In particular, the framework predicts the start of arc faults at 0.189 seconds, significantly earlier of the actual fault incidence at 0.200 seconds, demonstrating substantial early warning capability. Performance evaluation comprises eigenvalue analysis, prediction error measures, error growth rate and waveform regeneration fidelity. Such early prediction proves that the model is capable of correctly foreseeing faults which is especially helpful in preventing real-world faults and accidents. It confirms that our proposed approach reliably predicts arc faults in medium-voltage power distribution systems

AI.tldr: A data-driven linearization framework enables early prediction of high-impedance arc faults in medium-voltage systems by detecting invisible precursors from pre-fault healthy data.
AI.motivation: High-impedance arc faults are difficult to detect due to low fault currents and nonlinear transient behavior, and traditional algorithms struggle with dynamic waveform scenarios, creating a need for improved early detection methods.
AI.method: A unique data-driven linearization framework translates nonlinear current waveforms into a linearized space using coordinate embeddings and polynomial transformation, trained solely on pre-fault healthy data (0.10-0.18 seconds).
AI.result: The method predicts fault onset at 0.189 seconds, 11 milliseconds earlier than actual fault occurrence at 0.200 seconds, demonstrating substantial early warning capability with evaluation through eigenvalue analysis, prediction error measures, error growth rate, and waveform regeneration fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach reliably predicts arc faults in medium-voltage power distribution systems, enabling early warning that can prevent real-world faults and accidents by detecting invisible precursors from healthy pre-fault data.

title: SHINE: Sequential Hierarchical Integration Network for EEG and MEG
summary: How natural speech is represented in the brain constitutes a major challenge for cognitive neuroscience, with cortical envelope-following responses playing a central role in speech decoding. This paper presents our approach to the Speech Detection task in the LibriBrain Competition 2025, utilizing over 50 hours of magnetoencephalography (MEG) signals from a single participant listening to LibriVox audiobooks. We introduce the proposed Sequential Hierarchical Integration Network for EEG and MEG (SHINE) to reconstruct the binary speech-silence sequences from MEG signals. In the Extended Track, we further incorporated auxiliary reconstructions of speech envelopes and Mel spectrograms to enhance training. Ensemble methods combining SHINE with baselines (BrainMagic, AWavNet, ConvConcatNet) achieved F1-macro scores of 0.9155 (Standard Track) and 0.9184 (Extended Track) on the leaderboard test set.

AI.tldr: SHINE network for MEG-based speech detection achieves 0.9155-0.9184 F1 scores in LibriBrain Competition 2025 using hierarchical architecture and ensemble methods.
AI.motivation: Understanding how natural speech is represented in the brain is a major challenge in cognitive neuroscience, with cortical envelope-following responses being crucial for speech decoding. The LibriBrain Competition 2025 provides a platform to advance speech detection from MEG signals.
AI.method: Proposed Sequential Hierarchical Integration Network for EEG and MEG (SHINE) to reconstruct binary speech-silence sequences from MEG signals. In Extended Track, incorporated auxiliary reconstructions of speech envelopes and Mel spectrograms. Used ensemble methods combining SHINE with baselines (BrainMagic, AWavNet, ConvConcatNet).
AI.result: Achieved F1-macro scores of 0.9155 (Standard Track) and 0.9184 (Extended Track) on the leaderboard test set using over 50 hours of MEG data from a single participant listening to LibriVox audiobooks.
AI.conclusion: SHINE demonstrates effective speech detection from MEG signals, with ensemble methods and auxiliary reconstructions further improving performance, contributing to understanding neural speech representation.

title: Neural Luenberger state observer for nonautonomous nonlinear systems
summary: This work proposes a method for model-free synthesis of a state observer for nonlinear systems with manipulated inputs, where the observer is trained offline using a historical or simulation dataset of state measurements. We use the structure of the Kazantzis-Kravaris/Luenberger (KKL) observer, extended to nonautonomous systems by adding an additional input-affine term to the linear time-invariant (LTI) observer-state dynamics, which determines a nonlinear injective mapping of the true states. Both this input-affine term and the nonlinear mapping from the observer states to the system states are learned from data using fully connected feedforward multi-layer perceptron neural networks. Furthermore, we theoretically prove that trained neural networks, when given new input-output data, can be used to observe the states with a guaranteed error bound. To validate the proposed observer synthesis method, case studies are performed on a bioreactor and a Williams-Otto reactor.

AI.tldr: A data-driven method for synthesizing model-free state observers for nonlinear systems with inputs using neural networks to learn KKL observer structure with guaranteed error bounds.
AI.motivation: To develop a model-free approach for state observer synthesis that doesn't require knowledge of system dynamics, using only historical or simulation data, enabling state estimation for nonlinear systems with manipulated inputs.
AI.method: Extends Kazantzis-Kravaris/Luenberger (KKL) observer structure to nonautonomous systems by adding input-affine term to LTI observer-state dynamics. Learns both the input-affine term and nonlinear mapping from observer states to system states using fully connected feedforward multi-layer perceptron neural networks trained offline on historical/simulation data.
AI.result: Theoretical proof that trained neural networks can observe states with guaranteed error bounds when given new input-output data. Validation through case studies on bioreactor and Williams-Otto reactor demonstrates practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a novel data-driven observer synthesis method that combines theoretical KKL observer structure with neural network learning, providing guaranteed error bounds and demonstrating effectiveness on chemical engineering systems without requiring system model knowledge.

title: SongSong: A Time Phonograph for Chinese SongCi Music from Thousand of Years Away
summary: Recently, there have been significant advancements in music generation. However, existing models primarily focus on creating modern pop songs, making it challenging to produce ancient music with distinct rhythms and styles, such as ancient Chinese SongCi. In this paper, we introduce SongSong, the first music generation model capable of restoring Chinese SongCi to our knowledge. Our model first predicts the melody from the input SongCi, then separately generates the singing voice and accompaniment based on that melody, and finally combines all elements to create the final piece of music. Additionally, to address the lack of ancient music datasets, we create OpenSongSong, a comprehensive dataset of ancient Chinese SongCi music, featuring 29.9 hours of compositions by various renowned SongCi music masters. To assess SongSong's proficiency in performing SongCi, we randomly select 85 SongCi sentences that were not part of the training set for evaluation against SongSong and music generation platforms such as Suno and SkyMusic. The subjective and objective outcomes indicate that our proposed model achieves leading performance in generating high-quality SongCi music.

AI.tldr: SongSong is the first music generation model for restoring ancient Chinese SongCi, using a melody prediction and multi-stage generation approach with a newly created 29.9-hour dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing music generation models focus primarily on modern pop songs, making it difficult to produce ancient music with distinct rhythms and styles like Chinese SongCi. There is also a lack of dedicated datasets for ancient music.
AI.method: The model first predicts melody from input SongCi text, then separately generates singing voice and accompaniment based on that melody, and finally combines all elements to create the final music. The authors also created OpenSongSong, a comprehensive 29.9-hour dataset of ancient Chinese SongCi music.
AI.result: SongSong was evaluated on 85 unseen SongCi sentences against platforms like Suno and SkyMusic. Both subjective and objective results show that SongSong achieves leading performance in generating high-quality SongCi music.
AI.conclusion: SongSong represents the first successful music generation model for restoring ancient Chinese SongCi, addressing the gap in ancient music generation through a novel multi-stage approach and a newly created comprehensive dataset.

title: FaultXformer: A Transformer-Encoder Based Fault Classification and Location Identification model in PMU-Integrated Active Electrical Distribution System
summary: Accurate fault detection and localization in electrical distribution systems is crucial, especially with the increasing integration of distributed energy resources (DERs), which inject greater variability and complexity into grid operations. In this study, FaultXformer is proposed, a Transformer encoder-based architecture developed for automatic fault analysis using real-time current data obtained from phasor measurement unit (PMU). The approach utilizes time-series current data to initially extract rich temporal information in stage 1, which is crucial for identifying the fault type and precisely determining its location across multiple nodes. In Stage 2, these extracted features are processed to differentiate among distinct fault types and identify the respective fault location within the distribution system. Thus, this dual-stage transformer encoder pipeline enables high-fidelity representation learning, considerably boosting the performance of the work. The model was validated on a dataset generated from the IEEE 13-node test feeder, simulated with 20 separate fault locations and several DER integration scenarios, utilizing current measurements from four strategically located PMUs. To demonstrate robust performance evaluation, stratified 10-fold cross-validation is performed. FaultXformer achieved average accuracies of 98.76% in fault type classification and 98.92% in fault location identification across cross-validation, consistently surpassing conventional deep learning baselines convolutional neural network (CNN), recurrent neural network (RNN). long short-term memory (LSTM) by 1.70%, 34.95%, and 2.04% in classification accuracy and by 10.82%, 40.89%, and 6.27% in location accuracy, respectively. These results demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed model with significant DER penetration.

AI.tldr: FaultXformer: A dual-stage Transformer encoder architecture for fault detection and localization in electrical distribution systems using PMU current data, achieving ~99% accuracy and outperforming CNN/RNN/LSTM baselines.
AI.motivation: Accurate fault detection and localization is crucial in modern distribution systems due to increasing DER integration, which introduces greater variability and complexity into grid operations, necessitating advanced real-time analysis methods.
AI.method: Proposes FaultXformer, a Transformer encoder-based architecture with dual-stage processing: Stage 1 extracts temporal information from PMU current time-series data for fault identification; Stage 2 processes these features for fault type classification and location identification. Validated on IEEE 13-node test feeder with 20 fault locations and various DER scenarios using 4 PMUs, with stratified 10-fold cross-validation.
AI.result: Achieved average accuracies of 98.76% for fault type classification and 98.92% for fault location identification. Outperformed CNN, RNN, and LSTM baselines by 1.70%, 34.95%, 2.04% in classification and 10.82%, 40.89%, 6.27% in location accuracy respectively.
AI.conclusion: FaultXformer demonstrates superior performance for fault analysis in distribution systems with significant DER penetration, leveraging Transformer architecture's high-fidelity representation learning capabilities for accurate real-time fault detection and localization.

title: Curriculum-Based Soft Actor-Critic for Multi-Section R2R Tension Control
summary: Precise tension control in roll-to-roll (R2R) manufacturing is difficult under varying operating conditions and process uncertainty. This paper presents a curriculum-based Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) controller for multi-section R2R tension control. The policy is trained in three phases with progressively wider reference ranges, from 27 to 33 N to the full operating envelope of 20 to 40 N, so it can generalize across nominal and disturbed conditions. On a three-section R2R benchmark, the learned controller achieves accurate tracking in nominal operation and handles large disturbances, including 20 N to 40 N step changes, with a single policy and no scenario-specific retuning. These results indicate that curriculum-trained SAC is a practical alternative to model-based control when system parameters vary and process uncertainty is significant.

AI.tldr: Curriculum-trained Soft Actor-Critic controller for multi-section roll-to-roll tension control achieves accurate tracking across wide operating ranges and handles large disturbances without retuning.
AI.motivation: Precise tension control in roll-to-roll manufacturing is challenging due to varying operating conditions and process uncertainty, requiring robust control methods that can handle disturbances without extensive retuning.
AI.method: Curriculum-based Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) controller trained in three phases with progressively wider reference ranges (27-33 N → full 20-40 N envelope) to enable generalization across nominal and disturbed conditions.
AI.result: On a three-section R2R benchmark, the learned controller achieves accurate tracking in nominal operation and handles large disturbances including 20 N to 40 N step changes with a single policy and no scenario-specific retuning.
AI.conclusion: Curriculum-trained SAC is a practical alternative to model-based control for R2R tension control when system parameters vary and process uncertainty is significant, offering robust performance across wide operating ranges.

title: Observer-Based Estimation and Hydrostatic Inertia Modeling for Cooperative Transport of Variable-Inertia Loads with Quadrotors
summary:
We address load-parameter estimation in cooperative aerial transport with time-varying mass and inertia, as in fluid-carrying payloads. Using an intrinsic manifold model of the multi-quadrotor-load dynamics, we combine a geometric tracking controller with an observer for parameter identification. We estimate mass from measurable kinematics and commanded forces, and handle variable inertia via an inertia surrogate that reproduces the load's rotational dynamics for control and state propagation.
  Instead of real-time identification of the true inertia tensor, driven by high-dimensional internal fluid motion, we leverage known tank geometry and fluid-mechanical structure to pre-compute inertia tensors and update them through a lookup table indexed by fill level and attitude. The surrogate is justified via the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations in the translating/rotating load frame: when effective forcing is gravity-dominated (i.e., translational/rotational accelerations and especially jerk are limited), the fluid approaches hydrostatic equilibrium and the free surface is well approximated by a plane orthogonal to the body-frame gravity direction.

AI.tldr: Geometric approach for load parameter estimation in cooperative aerial transport with time-varying mass and inertia, using a surrogate inertia model based on fluid mechanics instead of real-time identification.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of load-parameter estimation for cooperative aerial transport systems carrying fluid payloads with time-varying mass and inertia, where real-time identification of the true inertia tensor is difficult due to complex internal fluid motion.
AI.method: Combine geometric tracking controller with observer for parameter identification using intrinsic manifold model of multi-quadrotor-load dynamics. Estimate mass from measurable kinematics and commanded forces. Handle variable inertia via pre-computed inertia surrogate based on known tank geometry and fluid-mechanical structure, updated through lookup table indexed by fill level and attitude.
AI.result: Developed a practical approach that avoids real-time identification of high-dimensional fluid motion by leveraging fluid-mechanical structure to create an inertia surrogate that reproduces the load's rotational dynamics for control and state propagation.
AI.conclusion: For gravity-dominated scenarios with limited accelerations and jerk, the fluid approaches hydrostatic equilibrium, allowing the free surface to be approximated by a plane orthogonal to body-frame gravity direction, justifying the surrogate inertia approach.

title: Virtual Constraint for a Quadrotor UAV Enforcing a Body-Axis Pointing Direction
summary: We propose a geometric control framework on $SE(3)$ for quadrotors that enforces pointing-driven missions without completing a full attitude reference. The mission is encoded through virtual constraints defining a task manifold and an associated set of admissible velocities, and invariance is achieved by a feedback law obtained from a linear system in selected inputs. Under a transversality condition with the effective actuation distribution, the invariance-enforcing input is uniquely defined, yielding a constructive control law and, for relevant tasks, closed-form expressions. We further derive a local off-manifold stabilization extension. As a case study, we lock a body axis to a prescribed line-of-sight direction while maintaining fixed altitude.

AI.tldr: Geometric control framework on SE(3) for quadrotors enabling pointing-driven missions without full attitude tracking, using virtual constraints to define task manifolds and admissible velocities.
AI.motivation: Many quadrotor missions require pointing a specific body axis (e.g., camera, sensor) toward a target while allowing rotational freedom around that axis, rather than tracking a complete attitude reference. This reduces control complexity and computational load.
AI.method: Formulate mission as virtual constraints defining a task manifold and admissible velocities on SE(3). Derive invariance-enforcing feedback law from linear system in selected inputs under transversality condition with effective actuation distribution. Provide constructive control law with closed-form expressions for relevant tasks, plus local off-manifold stabilization extension.
AI.result: Framework yields unique invariance-enforcing input under transversality condition, enabling constructive control design with closed-form solutions. Case study demonstrates locking body axis to prescribed line-of-sight while maintaining fixed altitude.
AI.conclusion: Geometric control on SE(3) provides systematic approach for pointing-driven quadrotor missions without full attitude tracking, offering computational efficiency and explicit control laws through task manifold formulation and invariance enforcement.
